segments.s063-g81: پذیرفتم is simple past, 1st person singular: 'I accepted / I agreed.' The phrase از دادگر داورم attaches as a prepositional phrase 'from the just/righteous arbiter' (از + epithet دادگر modifying داور + possessive/relational -م), so the whole segment means 'I accepted (it) from the just judge/arbiter.'
segments.s063-g82: که introduces a subordinate content clause here ('that'). هرگز is an adverb 'never.' ز (poetic form of از) + پیمان 'from / with the pledge' and تو 'you' mark the object/target of نگذرم. نگذرم is present-subjunctive/negative-conditional sense of گذشتن here meaning 'to pass/break (a pledge)' — 'I will not break/pass over (your) pledge.'
segments.s063-g83: شوم is an optative/subjunctive form of شدن ('may I become / may I be permitted'); پیش یزدان = 'in the presence of / before God.' ستایش کنم uses the present-subjunctive کن‑م 'may I do/praise' with ستایش (praise) as its object; together the clause expresses a wish: 'may I (be allowed to) praise before God.'
segments.s063-g84: چو is a comparative/concessive 'as / like.' ایزد پرستان is a compound plural 'God‑worshippers / devotees of God' (ایزد + پرستان). نیایش کنم places the noun نیایش 'prayer' before the verb کن‑م (subjunctive), yielding 'I will (or may) offer prayer,' mirroring the previous optative sense.
segments.s063-g85: مگر here functions rhetorically ('is it not that / perhaps'); کو is an older connective used like 'that/where' in such questions. دل سام و شاه زمین strings two appositive titles: 'the heart of Sam and (the) king of the land' (دل + proper name سام, and then شاه زمین 'king of the land'). The phrase sets up the subject for the following verb بشوید.
segments.s063-g86: بشوید is the present-subjunctive/passive-like form of شدن 'may/become' (here: 'be cleansed' / 'become rid of'). ز introduces the source/thing removed: ز خشم 'of anger', ز پیکار 'of battle', و کین 'and of hatred.' The sequence lists the things from which the heart should be freed.
segments.s063-g87: جهان‌آفرین is an epithet 'the world‑creator' (God). بشنود is a jussive/subjunctive verb 'may (he) hear.' گفت من literally 'my saying' (گفت as 'speech/words' with من as possessor) — a compact way to say 'may the Creator hear my words.'
segments.s063-g88: مگر again functions as an optative/rhetorical 'would that / may it be that.' کاشکارا is an adverb 'plainly / clearly.' شوی is subjunctive of شدن 'you become / you be.' جفت من 'my pair / my mate' (جفت = spouse/partner, modified by possessive من). Together: 'May you clearly become my mate.'
segments.s063-g89: بدو گفت رودابه من همچنین is a compact, somewhat inverted reporting clause: بدو (to him) is the indirect object placed before the verb گفت (said), which here functions as the reporting verb (past). The subject رودابه follows the verb (a poetic inversion), and من همچنین ('I likewise') is the quote-subject/contrastive phrase that prepares the direct statement that follows.
segments.s063-g90: پذیرفتم is first-person past/present-perfect meaning 'I accepted/consented.' The prepositional phrase از داور کیش و دین follows and indicates the authority/grounding of that acceptance: از = 'by/from' (approval of), داور here = the divine/ultimate judge, and کیش و دین = 'creed and religion' (an attributive pair describing the judge's domain).
segments.s063-g91: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining the condition/term just accepted. The sequence بر من نباشد کسی پادشا uses poetic inversion: نباشد (lit. 'be not' — negative subjunctive/optative here) precedes the logical subject کسی پادشا ('anyone/any king'), and بر من ('over me') marks the scope; the meaning is 'that no one be king over me.'
segments.s063-g92: جهان‌آفرین بر زبانم گوا is a short, semi-elliptical oath: جهان‌آفرین = 'the Creator of the world' is the one invoked; بر زبانم ('upon my tongue' = to my words) gives the domain of witnessing; گوا here functions as 'be witness'/'bear witness' (an archaic/poetic usage), so the whole clause means 'may the Creator of the world witness my words.'
segments.s063-g93: جز از marks an exception ('except for'). The noun phrase پهلوان جهان is a genitival grouping 'champion of the world.' زال زر is an appositive naming that champion — Zāl with the epithet زر ('gold'/'the Golden'). Word order is noun modifiers following heads in the usual Persian fashion.
segments.s063-g94: که introduces a relative clause describing Zal. با تخت و تاجست is 'with throne and crown' where تاجست is an archaic fused form (تاج + است) meaning '(is) crown/has a crown' — Persian poetic compression often fuses copula. The sequence وبا زیب و فر should be read as و با ('and with') زیب ('beauty') و فر ('and glory'); here orthography fuses و and با, so the clause lists Zal's possessions/qualities.
segments.s063-g95: همی is an emphatic/durative adverb meaning 'always' or 'ever'; مهرشان ('their love/affection') is the subject. هر زمان بیش بود literally 'was more at every time' — a comparative-past construction meaning their affection was ever the greater/was always abundant.
segments.s063-g96: This line gives two parallel predications: خرد دور بود ('wisdom was distant')—i.e. wisdom was lacking or remote—and آرزو پیش بود ('desire was foremost' / 'desire was ahead'), where پیش functions adverbially to mean 'in front/first.' The repeated بود is the past copula for each clause.
segments.s063-g97: چنین تا introduces a temporal clause: چنین (thus/so) + تا (until) marks the time span. سپیده (dawn) is the subject and برآمد (past tense) is the verbal predicate; ز جای is a simple prepositional phrase 'from (its) place' with ز (poetic 'from') before the noun جای. Word order here is slightly topicalized but follows normal Persian past narrative (subject + past verb).
segments.s063-g98: تبیره is the noun (here presented as the thing/person that 'rose'); برآمد is again the past verb 'rose/appeared'; ز + پرده‌سرای is the prepositional phrase 'from the curtain-room / stage'. The clause is a simple past action parallel to the previous clause (two things rising at dawn).
segments.s063-g99: پس is a narrative connector 'then'. آن ماه را is fronted and marked by the object marker را (topicalized/object); شید is the subject (a person/lover named or described as 'shid'), and پدرود کرد is the light verb construction 'bade greeting / greeted' (پدرود = greeting, کرد = did). Note the object/topic appears before the subject for emphasis.
segments.s063-g100: This is a figurative compound: بر (on/upon) + خوی + ش yields 'upon his own disposition/ himself' (خوی + ش = his own). تار and پود are technical weaving terms (warp and weft) coordinated by و. کرد is the main past verb 'did/made', meaning 'he set/laid the warp upon himself and placed the weft upon it'. Note that برش here functions as a prepositional phrase 'بر + (ـ)ش' ('upon it/him/her') with the possessive clitic realized; the printed segmentation treats برش as one token.
segments.s063-g101: ز بالا (from above) is a locative phrase. کمند اندر افگند is a past action 'cast/let down the lasso/snare' (افگند = threw/cast; اندر = in/into). The final زال is the proper name 'Zal' and syntactically connects to the next clause as the one who moves (the subject of فرود آمد). The clause omits an explicit agent for افگند (it is implied or generic).
segments.s063-g102: فرود آمد is a light/compound past meaning 'descended / alighted' (فرود nominal + آمد auxiliary-like verb). از + کاخ introduces the origin 'from the palace of ...', and فرخ همال is a name or epithet in genitive position modifying کاخ (here treated as 'Farrokh‑Hamal' or 'the fortunate porter'). The prepositional phrase follows the verb as the usual Persian order for source-of-motion.
segments.s063-g65: دو رخساره ('two cheeked faces') is a simple numeral + noun phrase; چون introduces a simile ('like'). اندر is the older/pre‑poetic locative 'in/among'. The clause reads literally: 'two faces like tulips in/among jasmine' with the verb omitted as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s063-g66: This is an appositive descriptive phrase. سر جعد = 'the head/crown of the curl' (سر + noun), زلفش = زلف + -ش (possessive suffix 'her'), and شکن بر شکن is a poetic repetition meaning 'breaker upon breaker' (شکن repeated for emphasis) with بر = 'upon'. There is no finite verb: the line piles visual epithets.
segments.s063-g67: همان is a demonstrative 'that/same' pointing to the named figure; زال is the proper name. با introduces an accompaniment/attribute ('with'); فر شاهنشهی is a genitive/compound: فر 'glory' qualified by شاهنشهی 'of kingship/imperial', so 'with the glory of imperial kingship.'
segments.s063-g68: نشسته is a past participle/adjectival predicate 'seated' used statively. It governs two prepositional phrases: بر ماه ('on/upon the moon' — moon used metaphorically for a beautiful face) and بر فرهی ('on his/the noble brow'); Persian poetry often allows this imagery where the 'moon' is said to 'sit on' a noble brow. The second بر repeats the preposition for rhythmic emphasis.
segments.s063-g69: حمایل here functions as a wearable/supporting item (scabbard/girdle) that holds something; یکی دشنه = 'one dagger/a certain dagger'. اندر introduces location 'in/within'. برش in context means 'on/at him' (the possessive/locative sense), an idiomatic elliptical formula in older Persian; the line describes that a single dagger lay at his side/in his girdle.
segments.s063-g70: ز + یاقوت سرخ is a poetic genitive/partitive: 'of (or from) ruby-red' qualifying the following noun. افسری is a noun meaning 'diadem/helmet/crest'; بر سرش ('upon his head') is a fixed locative phrase where سرش is سر + -ش (possessive 'his'). The whole phrase: 'a diadem of red ruby upon his head.'
segments.s063-g71: همی بود is a frequent poetic periphrasis meaning 'there was/there used to be' (continuous past/state). The nouns بوس و کنار و نبید are a coordinated list (kisses, companionship/attendance, and wine); Persian poetry often strings such nouns without individual verbs.
segments.s063-g72: مگر introduces an exception ('except/unless'). شیر کو uses کو as the archaic relative 'who/which'. گور را is the direct object 'the grave' (را marks definiteness/object). نشکرید here is an archaic/periphrastic past form meaning 'tore open/broke' (negative or exception sense in context); the line as a whole gives an aside: 'except where a lion had torn open the grave.' Note: نشکرید is an archaic/poetic verbal form and does not match the modern lemma senses listed, so sense index is set to -1.
segments.s063-g73: This is an inverted, somewhat archaic order: subject (سپهبد) + manner adverb (چنین) + past verb (گفت) + preposition with its object (با ماه‌روی). Here گفت functions as the speech verb 'spoke/said' and با introduces the person addressed (the moon‑faced one).
segments.s063-g74: This is a vocative/exclamatory phrase introduced by که‌ای (an emphatic/archaic form akin to 'O you'); سرو سیمین‌بر is a compound epithet ('cypress' + 'silver‑brow/with silver upon it') and رنگ بوی pairs two nouns (color/complexion and scent) as poetic attributes. The conjunction و simply links the two attributes.
segments.s063-g75: Standard conditional clause order: subject (منوچهر) + conditional particle (اگر) + verb (بشنود) + object (داستان). بشنود is a present/subjunctive form of 'to hear' used here for a conditional/hypothetical 'should hear' or simple 'he hears'.
segments.s063-g76: نباشد is an archaic negative existential/capability form meaning 'there will not be' or 'there is no' (here predicting absence). برین is the preposition بر + demonstrative/integrated ending 'ین' giving 'on/over this' (i.e. 'in this'), کار is 'affair/deed', and همداستان means 'like‑minded' or 'in agreement'—together: 'there will be no like‑minded person in this affair.'
segments.s063-g77: همان is a demonstrative 'that very', followed by the proper name سام. نیرم here is an epithet attached to سام (an archaic/poetic adjective whose exact nuance is irregular in the supplied senses). برآرد is a present/optative form meaning 'will raise/bring forth', and خروش is the noun 'a cry/uproar'—together: 'that same Sam will raise a cry.'
segments.s063-g78: ازین کار = 'from/out of this affair' (از + این with poetic fusion). بر من شود is an idiomatic passive/impersonal turn meaning 'be directed against me' ('بر' = against/toward + من + شود 'becomes'), and او بجوش uses بجوش (literally 'let it boil') metaphorically 'he will seethe/rage'—so: 'on account of this affair he will be against me, seething.'
segments.s063-g79: ولیکن is a literary 'but/however'. نه negates the following predicate. پرمایه is an adjective 'rich/substantial' qualifying جانست (an archaic fused form of جان است, here meaning 'is (of) life' or 'is dear/precious'), and و تن adds 'and the body'—i.e. 'but life and body are not of much value.'
segments.s063-g80: همان 'that same' modifies خوار ('despised/lowly one'). گیرم is a first‑person present/optative form meaning 'I will take' or 'I take' (classical/poetic mood), بپوشم is first‑person present/optative of پوشیدن 'I will clothe/cover' and کفن is the object 'shroud'—together: 'I will take that despised one and wrap him in a shroud.'
segments.s063-g49: به here is the preposition indicating motion 'into/toward' and precedes حلقه ('ring, circle'), which is the goal. درآمد is a past action 'entered/issued in' (verbal idea; the noun-listing of درآمد in the glosses functions here as the verbal sense 'entrance/entering'). The actual subject 'سر کنگره' (the head/top of the battlement) follows the verb for poetic inversion — more prosaically: سرِ کنگره به حلقه درآمد.
segments.s063-g50: برآمد is the past tense 'rose/issued forth'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' + بن 'base/foundation' marks the origin. تا introduces the extent 'as far as/until', followed by به سر 'to the top' and یکسره as an adverb 'completely / straight through' — together: 'it rose from the base up to the very summit without interruption.'
segments.s063-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بر + بام is the locative phrase 'on the roof' (بر = on). آن باره is a parenthetical noun phrase 'that occasion/that time'. بنشست is the simple past 'sat down'; باز is the adverb 'again' placed after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s063-g52: برآمد is again the past 'arose/appeared' with the subject پری روی ('fair-faced fairy' or 'beautiful-faced maiden') where پری (fairy/beautiful woman) modifies روی ('face'). و links the clauses. بردش is the verb بردن with the enclitic object -ش 'him/her' meaning 'took/seized (him/her)'; نماز (literally 'prayer') functions here idiomatically/metaphorically as the thing that seizes or overwhelms — 'prayer/reverence overtook him.' Grammatically, بردش is transitive with نماز as its object in this idiomatic usage.
segments.s063-g53: گرفت is the past verb 'seized/took'. آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that moment/then'. The phrase دست دستان is a doubled/emphatic construction where دست 'hand' and دستان 'hands' indicate grasping the hands (or 'the hands of Dastān' in other contexts); به دست is an instrumental/locative phrase 'into/in his hand' or 'with the hand'. Word order is poetic but the sense is 'he took hold of the hands.'
segments.s063-g54: برفتند is the past plural 'they went off/they departed'. هر دو is 'both' (each of the two). به کردار is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in the manner of' and مست is an adjective 'drunk/intoxicated' used here figuratively to describe their behavior — 'they went off both behaving like the intoxicated.'
segments.s063-g55: فرود آمد is a compound verbal idea 'descended' (فرود = descent + آمد = came) functioning as the past action. از marks origin 'from', followed by بام 'roof' and کاخ بلند 'lofty/high palace' where بلند is the adjective modifying کاخ. Word order places the location after the verb as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s063-g56: به introduces the destination 'onto/to'. دست اندرون is an idiomatic compound meaning 'the inner hand/inner quarters' (اندرون = inner part/inner court) — here indicating the inner courtyard or inner area reached. The second دست repeats the notion of a 'hand/arm' and is syntactically linked to شاخ بلند 'a tall bough/branch' (شاخ = branch, بلند = tall), so the phrase describes alighting onto a long/tall bough that extends into the inner court. Grammatically, the sequence is preposition + locative phrase + noun phrase describing what he landed on.
segments.s063-g17: سپهبد is the subject (title) placed at the start. کزان گونه آوا is an adjectival/adv. phrase meaning “a sound of that sort/ in that manner” (کزان = “from/of which”, گونه = “manner/type”, آوا = “sound/voice”). شنید is the simple past 3rd-person singular (“he heard”) and completes the clause; the whole order is Subject + descriptive phrase + past verb, typical of literary Persian.
segments.s063-g18: نگه کرد is a periphrastic verbal phrase: نگه (gaze/watch) + کرد (past of کردن) = “he looked / fixed his gaze.” The conjunction و links this action to the next. خورشید رخ را places the modifier خورشید before رخ (“sun-like face” or “face like the sun”), را is the definite/object marker, and بدید is the past 3rd-person singular (“he saw”), with the old literary prefix ب- visible.
segments.s063-g19: شده is a past participle/past result meaning “has become / was made,” followed by بام (roof) in inverted, poetic order: “the roof had become…”. از آن introduces the material/source (“from that”), and گوهر تابناک is a noun + adjective “that shining gem.” The clause thus states the roof’s material/state in past tense.
segments.s063-g20: به جای is a prepositional expression meaning “in place of / instead of.” گل سرخ is a compound “red flower/rose” (گل modified by سرخ). یاقوت خاک is a poetic genitival or compound where یاقوت (“ruby”) qualifies خاک (“dust/earth”), hence “ruby-dust.” There is no finite verb here because this phrase continues and specifies what replaced the flowers.
segments.s063-g21: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so.” داد is the past of دادن (“gave”) and takes پاسخ (“answer”) as its object: “gave an answer.” The particle که introduces the reported speech that follows; ای (attached to که here as که‌ای) is the vocative marker (“O”), so ماه چهر is the direct address “O moon-faced (one).”
segments.s063-g22: درودت is درود (greeting/blessing) with the enclitic -ت marking the addressee (“greeting to you”). ز من (“from me”) specifies the source of that greeting. آفرین here means “acclamation/praise,” and از سپهر (“from the heavens/sky”) gives its source; the clause is elliptical (a copula like باشد is omitted) and functions as the content of the reply.
segments.s063-g23: چه مایه is an exclamative quantifier meaning “what an amount/what a…,” functioning like “what a sight.” شبان (shepherds) and دیده (eyes) are juxtaposed as possessor + possessed (“the shepherds’ eyes”). اندر is the preposition “in,” and سماک here names the heaven/sky or a poetic celestial place; the whole clause is an exclamation about what the shepherds saw.
segments.s063-g24: خروشان is an adjective/participle meaning “roaring/tumultuous.” بدم is a first‑person past form in this poetic context meaning roughly “I was / I came” (here roughly “I rushed/appeared”); the construction is terse and classical. پیش یزدان پاک is a prepositional phrase “before / in the presence of the pure God,” with یزدان پاک = “the pure God.” Together the clause reports the speaker’s roaring approach before God.
segments.s063-g41: نگه کرد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'looked/gazed' (نگه = gaze + کرد = did/made), with the finite past verb کرد placed after نگه; زال is the subject following the verb (typical poetic word order). اندران is an older/poetic locative = 'in/at that' and governs ماه روی, literally 'moon-face' (ماه + روی = face like the moon), so the whole segment means 'Zal looked at that moonlike face.'
segments.s063-g42: شگفتی بماند is a verbal phrase 'astonishment remained/lingered' where بماند (ب‑ماند) here functions as a narrative perfect/imperfective 'remained'. اندران again = 'at/in that' links to روی و موی = 'face and hair' (روی = face, موی = hair). The structure is nominative (شگفتی) + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s063-g43: چنین داد پاسخ is 'thus gave an answer' (چنین = thus; داد here is the verb 'gave' used with پاسخ = 'answer'). که introduces reported speech. این نیست داد literally 'this is not داد' — داد in the second occurrence is the noun 'justice/right' (a different sense from the first داد), so the clause reports 'this is not right.'
segments.s063-g44: چنین روز = 'such a day'; خورشید روشن is a verbless nominal phrase 'the sun (be) bright' and مباد is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'may ... never happen'. Together this segment is a wish/curse: 'may the sun not be bright on such a day.'
segments.s063-g45: که introduces a content clause ('that...'). من is the subject; دست را is a definite, object-marked noun phrase ('the hand' with را for emphasis/definiteness). خیره here is an adjectival/adverbial use meaning 'fixed/firm' (poetic use related to 'staring' but functioning adverbially), بر جان = 'against/on the life' and زنم is first‑person present/subjunctive of زدن used in a hortatory/future sense 'I will strike' (poetic present used for future action).
segments.s063-g46: برین = بر + این ('upon this'); ین here is the orthographic/poetic variant of 'این'. خسته‌دل ('heart‑weary') is the target (the lovesick one). تیز پیکان is a noun phrase 'sharp arrows' and زنم repeats the 1st person verb 'I strike/shoot' — the clause says 'upon this lovesick one I shall shoot sharp arrows.' Word order places the prepositional/target phrase up front for emphasis.
segments.s063-g47: کمند از رهی بستد is a past simple clause: کمند (snare/lasso) is the object, از رهی = 'from (a) wayfarer' or 'from the road' (classical/genitive usage), and بستد is the past form 'tied/fastened'. و داد خم uses داد as a verb 'gave/did' with خم 'bend' (i.e. 'he bent/drew the bow'); here داد = 'did/gave' is used to indicate causing the bend.
segments.s063-g48: بیفگند is a past/transitive verb 'he threw/cast' (ب‑ prefix + افگند). خوار is an adjective/adverbial 'lowly/despised' or 'in scorn', so 'he cast (it) scornfully/low'. و نزد ایچ دم is a terse classical phrase: نزد = 'near' or 'before', ایچ = 'nothing/none' (classical negative), دم = 'breath/moment' — together meaning 'and there was not even a breath/instant near' (i.e. no response or respite).
segments.s063-g9: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause; از دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar' placed immediately after the conjunction. دستان here is a poetic epithet attached to the proper name phrase سام سوار (سام = the name, سوار = 'rider'); the sequence places the epithet before the named person for emphasis ('strong‑handed Sam, the rider').
segments.s063-g10: پدید آمد is a compound verbal expression meaning 'appeared' (پدید = appear/manifest + آمد = came) with آمد in the past tense. The subject آن دختر نامدار ('that renowned maiden') follows the verb here — a common poetic word order inversion in Persian where the verb precedes the emphatic subject.
segments.s063-g11: بگشاد is the past form of گشودن 'to open' (here with the archaic/poetic prefix ب-), so the clause means 'she opened/drew aside'. بیجاده is an adjectival phrase modifying the hidden/understood objects (literally 'without a path' / 'off the usual way' in archaic use), and آواز داد is a light verb construction 'gave a voice' = 'cried out/called'. The coordinating و links the two actions.
segments.s063-g12: که introduces direct speech or a quoted exclamation ('that/that is'): شاد آمدی literally 'you came happy' (شاد 'happy/merry' placed before the verb for topical/emphatic effect). ای is the vocative particle 'O', and جوانمرد is the addressee 'gallant/ chivalrous man'; the final شاد repeats the exclamation 'happy!' — a poetic doubling for emphasis.
segments.s063-g13: درود 'greeting' is the noun being wished; جهان‌آفرین 'World‑Creator' is in apposition (a title for the divine). بر تو باد is an optative formula: بر = 'upon', تو = 'you', and باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)', so together 'may ... be upon you.'
segments.s063-g14: This is a nominal wish: خم ('bend/curve') modifies چرخ ('wheel'), گردان is an attributive adjective/participle 'turning/revolving', and زمین is the genitive 'of the earth' modifying the whole phrase. The clause closes again with تو باد — 'may it be yours' using the optative باد.
segments.s063-g15: پیاده is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'on foot'. بدین سان is the idiom 'in this way/thus' (here composed as the prefix ب- + دین + سان in the poetic spelling), and ز پرده سرای is a prepositional phrase 'from behind the curtain of the chamber' (ز = 'from', پرده 'curtain', سرای 'chamber/room'). Together they set manner and source for the following action.
segments.s063-g16: برنجیدت is a past tense verb directed at the second person (the -ت ending = 'you'); in context it conveys the idea 'you were startled/roused' (an older/poetic verb form). این خسروانی دو پای is the noun phrase 'these two royal feet' (این 'this/these', خسروانی 'royal', دو پای literally 'two feet'), which functions as the agent/subject of the startling action.
segments.s063-g57: سوی is the preposition 'toward/to' placed before its noun phrase. خانه زرنگار is the noun ('house') followed by a postpositive adjective 'زرنگار' (poetic/compound adjective 'gilded' or 'gold‑decorated') — Persian poetry often puts adjectives after the noun. آمدند is the 3rd person plural past 'they came' (subject is omitted/pronounless, supplied by context).
segments.s063-g58: بران here is a fused prepositional phrase (بر + آن) meaning 'to/at that' or 'upon that', introducing the destination. مجلس شاهوار is 'assembly/court' (مجلس) qualified by the adjective شاهوار 'regal/lofty' following the noun. آمدند repeats the 3rd person plural past verb for emphasis (a common poetic device).
segments.s063-g59: بهشتی is an adjective/noun 'heavenly' or 'paradisiacal' describing the scene. بد آراسته is a compound where آراسته is the past‑participle 'adorned'; بد here functions as an archaic/intensifying modifier (not the usual negative meaning) to produce 'richly/beautifully adorned' — a poetic usage. پر ز نور is a fixed poetic structure meaning 'full of light' (پر = 'full', followed by the poetic preposition ز = 'of/from' + نور 'light').
segments.s063-g60: پرستنده is a noun 'worshipper/devotee' (here plural/collective by sense). It is followed by two prepositional phrases linked by و: بر پای 'at/on the (their) feet' and بر پیش حور 'before/in the presence of the houris' — بر is the preposition 'upon/at' and پیش here means 'in the presence of.' The verb is elliptical (understood from the previous clause or narrative).
segments.s063-g61: شگفت here functions as the noun 'wonder/astonishment' and اندرو (در او) means 'in him' or 'within him'; مانده is the past participle 'remained/was left' — together 'astonishment remained within him' = 'he was astonished'. بد is used as an archaic intensifying particle (not the everyday 'bad') modifying the reaction; زال is the proper name 'Zāl'; زر is 'gold' and here names what caused his astonishment. The clause is somewhat elliptical (common in epic poetry): the astonishment is stated, the cause follows.
segments.s063-g62: برآن is the prepositional/locative 'at/with that' or 'about that', introducing a string of accusative/affected nouns: روی 'face', آن موی 'that hair' (آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying موی), then بالا 'height/loftiness' and فر 'royal radiance/glory'. The nouns are coordinated by و; they enumerate the features that caused Zāl's astonishment.
segments.s063-g63: This segment lists garments/ornaments joined by و: ابا and یاره are archaic garment/ornament words (here names of cloak/girdle or similar items) — they are coordinated with طوق 'collar/necklace'. Then با + گوشوار uses the preposition با 'with' to attach 'earring' (گوشوار). The structure is a classic poetic inventory of dress and jewelry; several item names are archaic, so common modern senses may not cover them.
segments.s063-g64: ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/from' (marking material or adornment) governing 'دینار و گوهر' (dinar coins and gems). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', and باغ بهار is a noun‑noun collocation 'garden of spring' = 'spring garden' — the simile compares the jeweled ornaments to a blooming spring garden.
segments.s063-g25: همی is a durative/adverbial particle meaning 'still/kept/continually' used before the verb; خواستم is the first-person past of 'خواستن' meaning 'I wished/desired.' تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that/so that' (not temporal here). خدای جهان is the subject of that subordinate clause ('the God/Lord of the world'). Word order: main clause (همی خواستم) + تا + subordinate clause whose verb appears next.
segments.s063-g26: نماید is the classical third-person singular form (optative/jussive/present) of نماياندن used here as 'may/would show' (the verb of the تا-clause). م + را represent the object clitic مرا ('me') split in the segmentation: م = 'me' clitic and را = accusative/definite object marker attaching to it. رویت is 'your face' (رو + -ت possessive). اندر نهان is a prepositional phrase 'in secret' (اندر = 'in/within', نهان = 'hidden'). Literal order: 'may he show me your face in secret.'
segments.s063-g27: کنون is an adverb 'now.' شاد گشتم is a verb phrase: شاد (predicative adjective 'happy/joyful') + گشتم (past of گشتن, 'I became') = 'I became joyful.' به آواز تو is an adverbial of cause/means: به + آواز = 'at/by/through the voice (call) of' with تو = 'your.'
segments.s063-g28: بدین is an adverbial/heavy-prepositional form meaning 'by/with this' or 'in this way' (here 'by these words'); in older/poetic Persian it is a fused ب + این/دین. خوب‌گفتار is an epithet 'pleasant/eloquent-speaking' (= 'fine words' or 'eloquent speaker') describing the manner. با = 'with' and ناز تو = 'your coquetry/coyness' (ناز + تو). The whole segment modifies how the speaker became joyful.
segments.s063-g29: یکی = 'one' / 'a certain' used here as 'some' or 'at least one.' چاره = 'remedy/means/solution.' راه دیدار = 'the way/path of meeting' (راه = path/way, دیدار = meeting/seeing). جوی is the imperative/hortative form of جستن ('seek') used to command or urge: 'seek (a way).' The phrase thus reads 'seek some means/way for a meeting.'
segments.s063-g30: چه introduces a question ('what/why'). پرسی here is the second-person present 'you ask' (classical present form). تو is the pronoun 'you' (postverbal emphasis). بر باره = 'about the matter/occasion' (بر = on/upon/about, باره = occasion/matter). و = 'and'; من = 'I'; به کوی = prepositional phrase 'in/to the quarter/street' (به + کوی). The clause is roughly 'Why do you ask about that, while I am in the quarter.'
segments.s063-g31: پری‌روی is a compound epithet: پری = 'fairy/beautiful' used as 'beautiful (woman)' and روی = 'face' — together 'the fair-faced (one).' گفت is the past third-person 'said/spoke.' سپهبد is a title 'spahbed' (a military commander); شنود is a classical third-person form of شنیدن meaning 'he heard' (historical/past or present-final sense depending on context). The line has two coordinated clauses: the fair‑faced one spoke, and the spahbed heard.
segments.s063-g32: سر شعر = 'the head/opening of the poem' (سر = 'head/first part', شعر = 'poem/verse') with گلنار the name of the poem or addressee ('Gulnār'). بگشاد is an archaic past form meaning 'opened/unfolded' (prefix بـ + گشاد/گشود). زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the verb. Together: 'he quickly opened/uttered the opening verse of Gulnār.'
segments.s063-g33: کمندی گشاد is a noun (کمندی, 'a coil/loop/lasso') followed by its adjective (گشاد, 'broad/wide') without the colloquial ezāfe — a poetic compression common in Ferdowsi. او immediately after this phrase is the independent pronoun acting as the possessor ('her' / 'of her') referring back to the beloved; ز + سرو بلند ('from/of the tall cypress') gives the source or simile (her coil likened to a tall cypress).
segments.s063-g34: کس here is used negatively in sense 'no one' (an indefinite negative reading). از + مشک marks material or metaphorical 'of/from musk' (i.e. a noose made of musk); زان سان ('in that way/so') is a fixed poetic combination meaning 'in that manner'; نپیچد is the negative present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن ('to twist, to wind'), used here generically ('no one can/will wind'); کمند repeats the noun for emphasis ('a noose/coil'). The clause says no one can fashion/wind such a coil of musk in that way.
segments.s063-g35: خم اندر خم is a reduplicative poetic pattern meaning 'a bend within a bend' or 'coil upon coil' (اندر = 'in/within'). The following و introduces a second stacked image: مار بر مار بر uses مار ('snake') with repeated بر ('upon') to produce the image 'snake upon snake' or 'serpent upon serpent' — repetition and the preposition بر stack the images tightly (typical of epic descriptiveness).
segments.s063-g36: بران is an anaphoric adverb meaning 'thereon' / 'upon that', linking this clause to the previous images. غبغبش is غبغب ('double chin, jowl') with the possessive suffix -ش ('her/his'); نار بر نار بر repeats 'fire upon fire' with بر again stacking images for intensification. The whole segment gives a piled-up image: upon that jowl are flames, piled one upon another.
segments.s063-g37: بدو گفت is the poetic order for به او گفت ('he said to him'); بدو is the indirect-object pronoun ('to him'). بر تاز is an imperative-like phrase: بر + تاز(یدن) = 'set upon/charge at' (تاز used here as the root meaning vigorous attack). و connects the next command; برکش is a verbal imperative form (poetic compression of برکشیدن, 'raise/lift up'), and میان ('mid/among') indicates the place where the raising/striking should occur ('in the midst'). The clause is a sequence of imperatives, reported speech introduced by 'he said to him'.
segments.s063-g38: بر شیر بگشای is another imperative cluster: بر + شیر ('at/on the lion') and بگشای (بگشاییدن/بگشودن) 'open/strike/lay open' — here 'smite/strike the lion' is intended. و links the next object, چنگ کیان: چنگ can mean 'claw' or 'harp', and in this martial image it reads best as 'claw/talon'; کیان is the genitive plural 'of the kings' (the Kayanian dynasty), so the phrase evokes attacking both the lion and the royal (metaphorical) claw. Word order is typical Persian reported imperative style.
segments.s063-g39: بگیر is the imperative 'take!'. این سیه گیسو is a noun phrase 'this black tress/lock of hair' (سیه = 'black/dark', گیسو = 'tress/lock'). از یک سوم ('of one third') indicates quantity — she offers a portion (one third) of that tress; the poetic phrasing omits some connective words but the meaning is 'take one third of this black tress.'
segments.s063-g40: ز بهر تو is a prepositional phrase 'for your sake/for you' (ز = 'for/of', بهر = 'for/sake'). باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary', and همی is an emphatic particle here ('indeed/so'), common in epic poetry. گیسوم is گیسو with the 1st-person possessive suffix -م 'my tress(s)/my hair'; together the clause says 'for your sake my hair must indeed be (thus/divided).' The verb 'be' is implied, a normal poetic ellipsis.
segments.s063-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” خورشید تابنده is a noun (خورشید) followed by its adjective (تابنده) — “the shining sun.” شد is the past/copulative verb “became” used here to form an intransitive perfective (i.e. “became/was”), and ناپدید is a predicate adjective/participial complement meaning “vanished/gone,” so the whole chunk reads “when the shining sun became/was vanished” → “when the shining sun vanished.”
segments.s063-g2: در حجره is a locative prepositional phrase (“in/inside the chamber”). بستند is past tense, 3rd‑person plural “they closed/locked.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” گم شد is a two‑word verbal expression with گم “lost” and شد as the verbal auxiliary “became/was,” and note the poetic inversion here: the verb phrase گم شد precedes its subject کلید, so the clause reads “and the key became lost” (i.e. “the key was lost”).
segments.s063-g3: پرستنده شد is a noun (پرستنده) plus the past verb شد “became,” meaning “became a worshipper/devotee.” سوی is the directional preposition “toward(s),” and دستان سام is a noun phrase where دستان is taken here as ‘hands’ (idiomatically ‘in the hands of’) with سام as the proper name — i.e. “toward Sam’s hands / toward Sam.”
segments.s063-g4: که introduces a relative clause (“who/which”). شد is the past copula “became/was.” ساخته is a past participle/adjective “made/constructed” modifying کار (here meaning “deed/affair”), so ساخته کار = “a contrived/made deed.” بگذار (literally “put/place”) and گام (“step”) form a short verbal phrase meaning “laid/put down a step” — in poetry this can mean “proceeded/took a step.” The sequence is somewhat compressed/poetic: “who had made a deed and put down a step.”
segments.s063-g5: سپهبد is the subject (a title — spahbed/lieutenant‑general). سوی کاخ is the directional phrase “toward the palace.” بنهاد is a past verb meaning “set/placed” or “turned” in this context, and روی functions as the object “face” — together بنهاد روی = “turned his face.” The construction is a poetic word order: subject + direction + verb + object.
segments.s063-g6: چنان چون is a correlative comparative (“so/as”), followed by بود (past of ‘to be’) giving a comparative sense. مردم is the subject ‘people,’ while جفت جوی is a compound: جفت “pair” + جوی “seeking/search” → “seeking a mate.” Note the verb بود appears before the subject (poetic inversion): the phrase means essentially “just as people were seeking pairs.”
segments.s063-g7: برآمد is the past intransitive verb “arose/ascended.” سیه چشم is an epithet composed of adjective سیه “dark/black” + noun چشم “eye(s)” = “dark‑eyed.” گلرخ here is the name/appellative of the woman. به بام is a locative/directional phrase “onto the roof.” So the clause reads “Golrukh, dark‑eyed, arose onto the roof.”
segments.s063-g8: چو introduces a simile “as/like.” سرو سهی is a poetic image: سرو “cypress” + سهی (here “dark/black”) — “like a dark cypress.” بر + سرش is the preposition “on/upon” + possessive “her head.” ماه تام is a poetic compound “perfect/complete moon,” i.e. a full moon or a flawlessly beautiful moon‑like face. The line thus says that, like a dark cypress, the perfect moon was upon her head (a metaphor for a radiant head/face).

sentences.s063-s41: I accepted (this) from the righteous arbiter — that I will never break my pledge to you.
sentences.s063-s42: May I be (so) before God as to offer praise — like the devotees of God I will offer prayer.
sentences.s063-s43: Is it not that the heart of Sam, the king of the land, be cleansed of anger, of battle and of hatred?
sentences.s063-s44: May the Creator hear my words — may you clearly become my mate.
sentences.s063-s45: Rudabeh said to him, "I likewise accept it, by the judge of creed and religion."
sentences.s063-s46: that no king shall be over me; may the Creator of the world be witness to my words.
sentences.s063-s47: Except for the champion of the world, Zal the Golden, who has throne and crown and (has) beauty and glory.
sentences.s063-s48: Their love was always greater at every hour; wisdom was remote, desire was foremost.
sentences.s063-s49: Thus, until the dawn rose from its place, Tabireh rose from behind the curtain of the stage.
sentences.s063-s50: Then Shid greeted that moon; he set the warp on himself and (on it) wove the weft.
sentences.s063-s51: From above a lasso was cast in; Zal descended from the palace of Farrokh-Hamal.
sentences.s063-s33: Two cheeked faces, like tulips among jasmine; the crown of her curl — breaker upon breaker.
sentences.s063-s34: That Zal, with the splendour of imperial kingship, had a moonlike face seated upon his noble brow.
sentences.s063-s35: At his girdle a dagger was sheathed; a diadem of red ruby lay upon his head.
sentences.s063-s36: There was always kissing, closeness and wine—except where a lion had torn open the grave.
sentences.s063-s37: The spahbed thus spoke to the moon‑faced one: "O cypress with a silver brow and of color and scent!"
sentences.s063-s38: If Manuchehr hears the tale, there will be no like‑minded person in this affair.
sentences.s063-s39: That very Sam will raise a cry; because of this affair he will come against me, seething with rage.
sentences.s063-s40: But life and body are not precious; I will take that same lowly one and clothe (him) in a shroud.
sentences.s063-s25: The summit of the battlement came into the ring; it rose from the base all the way up to the very top.
sentences.s063-s26: When he sat again on that roof, a fair-faced fairy arose, and prayer overwhelmed him.
sentences.s063-s27: At that moment he took hold of her hands; the two of them went away, each behaving like the drunken.
sentences.s063-s28: He descended from the roof of the lofty palace onto a tall bough that reached into the inner court.
sentences.s063-s9: The spahbed heard such a sound; he fixed his gaze and saw the sun-like face.
sentences.s063-s10: The roof had become of that shining jewel; in place of red flowers there was ruby-dust.
sentences.s063-s11: Thus he answered: “O moon-faced one, greetings to you from me and acclamation from the heavens.”
sentences.s063-s12: What a spectacle the shepherds’ eyes beheld in the sky — I rushed forward roaring before the pure God.
sentences.s063-s21: Zal gazed at that moonlike face; astonishment remained at that face and hair.
sentences.s063-s22: He replied thus: 'This is not right — may the sun never shine on such a day.'
sentences.s063-s23: That I will set my hand upon life and strike; upon this lovesick one I will shoot sharp arrows.
sentences.s063-s24: He fastened a snare from the roadway and bent (drew) the bow; he cast (it) down in scorn, and there was not a breath/instant near.
sentences.s063-s5: When from afar Sam the rider — strong of hand — appeared, that renowned maiden came into view.
sentences.s063-s6: She drew aside two (veils) and uttered a call: 'Gladly you have come — O gallant one, glad!'
sentences.s063-s7: May the Creator of the World’s greeting be upon you; may the bending of the earth’s revolving wheel be yours.
sentences.s063-s8: On foot, in this manner, from behind the chamber’s curtain — these two royal feet startled you.
sentences.s063-s29: They came toward the gilded house; to that lofty royal assembly they came.
sentences.s063-s30: A heavenly place, richly adorned and full of light; worshippers (stood) at the feet and before the houris.
sentences.s063-s31: Zāl stood astonished at the gold — at that face and that hair, and at the height and radiance.
sentences.s063-s32: With cloaks, girdles and collars, and with earrings of dinars and gems — like a spring garden.
sentences.s063-s13: I kept wishing that the Lord of the world would show me your face in secret.
sentences.s063-s14: Now I became joyful at your voice, by these fine words and your coyness.
sentences.s063-s15: Seek some means or a way of meeting; why do you ask about the matter while I am in the quarter?
sentences.s063-s16: The fair‑faced one spoke; the spahbed heard, and he quickly opened the opening verse of Gulnār.
sentences.s063-s17: Her broad coil, like a lofty cypress; no one can twist a noose of musk in that manner.
sentences.s063-s18: Curve within curve, and snake upon snake; on that double‑chin there is fire upon fire.
sentences.s063-s19: He said to him, 'Charge upon him and raise (it) amid them; strike at the lion, and the Kayanian claw.'
sentences.s063-s20: Take this black tress — a third of it; for your sake my hair must indeed be (so).
sentences.s063-s1: When the shining sun vanished, they shut [her/it] in the chamber and the key was lost.
sentences.s063-s2: She became a devotee toward Dastan of Sam; who had contrived a deed and put down a step.
sentences.s063-s3: The spahbed turned his face toward the palace, just as people searching for mates were (doing).
sentences.s063-s4: The dark‑eyed Golrukh rose onto the roof; like a dark cypress, a perfect moon rested upon her head.
